figures.gloss: Gaulish 'Thunderer', wheel in hand — his name is simply the Celtic word for thunder.
figures.facet: His identity is exhausted by name and instrument — 'Thunder' who carries a wheel — unlike narratively rich thunderers (Thor, Perkūnas) no myth-cycle of Taranis survives, only cult epithet and iconography.
figures.provenance: Attested epigraphically across Gaul, Britain, and the Rhineland; linked to Jupiter via interpretatio Romana; wheel iconography catalogued in Miranda Green, The Gods of the Celts (1986).

figures.gloss: Shape-shifting goddess of battle, fate, and death among the Túatha Dé Danann, who decides who falls and washes the bloodied arms of the doomed at the ford; medieval sources sometimes group her with Badb and Macha as a trio of related war-goddesses rather than treating the names as strictly identical.
figures.facet: This figure anchors the best-attested member of the trio; Badb and Macha are held distinct rather than merged onto a single name as decoration.
figures.provenance: Cath Maige Tuired; Lebor na hUidre (Book of the Dun Cow, 12th c. compilation); the triad question surveyed in Rosalind Clark, The Great Queens: Irish Goddesses from the Morrígan to Cathleen Ní Houlihan (1991).

figures.gloss: Smith of the Túatha Dé Danann whose weapons never miss, and whose ale grants the gods their deathlessness.
figures.facet: Divine craftsmanship as literal life-support: it is Goibniu's ale, not a sacred spring or fruit, that keeps the Túatha Dé from aging — smithcraft folded directly into the mechanism of immortality.
figures.provenance: Cath Maige Tuired; Sanas Cormaic; grouped with fellow craftsmen Credne (metalworker) and Luchta (wright) as the Túatha Dé's trecheng of makers.

figures.gloss: Named ancestral figure of the Túatha Dé Danann ('the peoples of the goddess Danu'); she appears only as the genitive in that tribal name and a mention of her sons in Lebor Gabála Érenn — no independent myth of her survives, and her character is substantially a scholarly reconstruction from the name itself.
figures.facet: The corpus's clearest case of a goddess reconstructed backward from a genealogy's grammar rather than attested forward from any narrative about her.
figures.provenance: Lebor Gabála Érenn (Book of Invasions, 11th c. compilation); the reconstruction is treated critically in general surveys such as Mark Williams, Ireland's Immortals (2016).

figures.gloss: Lugh Lámhfhada ('Long-Arm'), the samildánach ('equally skilled in many arts') of the Túatha Dé Danann; he masters every craft claimed by Tara's doorkeepers and is admitted where single-skill specialists are turned away, then leads the Túatha Dé to victory over the Fomorians at the second battle of Mag Tuired.
figures.facet: As samildánach he embodies sovereignty-through-totality rather than a single domain — the test he passes at Tara's door is not what he can do but whether any craft can be found he lacks.
figures.provenance: Cath Maige Tuired (Second Battle of Mag Tuired, medieval redaction of earlier material); Lughnasadh recorded in Sanas Cormaic and later folklore; Gaulish Lugus attested epigraphically (Lyon/Lugdunum, Leiden) and named 'Gaulish Mercury' by Caesar, De Bello Gallico VI.17.

figures.gloss: King of the Fomorians, whose single baleful eye lays waste to armies when its lid is lifted; prophesied to die at his own grandson's hand, he imprisons his daughter Ethniu to prevent it, but Lugh — her son — kills him with a sling-stone at the second battle of Mag Tuired.
figures.facet: The chaoskampf made intergenerational: the monster is not felled by a stranger-hero but by the grandson he tried to forestall, folding the doomed-grandfather folk pattern into the god-slays-giant myth.
figures.provenance: Cath Maige Tuired; Lebor Gabála Érenn; later folklore at Tory Island, Co. Donegal ('Balor's Fort').

figures.gloss: 'The Good God' (Eochaid Ollathair) of the Túatha Dé Danann, wielding a club whose one end kills and whose other restores life, master of a cauldron from which none go unsatisfied, and possessor of a harp (Uaithne) commanding the order of the seasons and the moods of his people.
figures.facet: Chief-god-as-provider rather than chief-god-as-ruler: his power is measured in abundance dispensed — inexhaustible food, restored life, controlled seasons — not in dominion imposed.
figures.provenance: Cath Maige Tuired; Lebor Gabála Érenn; the club/cauldron/harp triad detailed across the medieval Irish mythological cycle (Book of Leinster compilation, 12th c.).

figures.gloss: Goddess of poetry (filidecht), smithcraft, and healing; Sanas Cormaic (Cormac's Glossary, c. 900) describes her as three sisters of one name, one presiding over each craft. Her feast, Imbolc (Feb 1), marks the year's first stirring of spring.
figures.facet: One of the clearest pagan-to-Christian continuities in Europe: her feast day, well-cults, and attributes (the perpetual flame at Kildare) transfer near-intact onto St Brigid of Kildare — a goddess surviving conversion by acquiring a hagiography.
figures.provenance: Sanas Cormaic; Lebor Gabála Érenn; the Kildare cult of St Brigid recorded by Gerald of Wales, Topographia Hibernica (12th c.).

figures.gloss: Antlered deity shown cross-legged, wearing or holding a torc, flanked by a ram-horned serpent and other beasts; named once, on the Pillar of the Boatmen (Paris, dedicated under Tiberius, 1st c. CE), and depicted unnamed on the Gundestrup cauldron's 'sorcerer' plate.
figures.facet: A god defined almost entirely by iconography, not narrative — no myth of him survives, only the recurring image of a beast-master seated among animals, making 'master of animals' the responsible reading rather than a reconstructed personality.
figures.provenance: Pillar of the Boatmen (Musée de Cluny, Paris); Gundestrup cauldron (Danish bog find, La Tène style, c. 2nd-1st c. BCE); Miranda Green, The Gods of the Celts (1986), surveying the iconography and cautioning against over-interpretation.

figures.gloss: Horse goddess and protector of riders, stables, and mules, depicted seated side-saddle among mares and foals or flanked by horses; the only Gaulish deity honored with her own festival at Rome, December 18, per the Fasti.
figures.facet: Her cult's spread by Roman cavalry auxiliaries turned a regional Gaulish goddess into the most widely attested deity of Roman Gaul — religion traveling along supply lines rather than conquest.
figures.provenance: Epigraphic corpus surveyed in Robert (1949) and subsequent CIL entries; Fasti Philocali (354 CE) records the December 18 festival.

figures.gloss: Sea god and Otherworld lord ('son of the Sea') of Irish and Manx tradition, owner of a boat needing no oars and a cloak of mist (féth fíada) that veils and divides worlds; the Isle of Man is said to preserve his name.
figures.facet: He governs passage rather than domain — his Otherworld is reached by crossing water or mist, making Manannán less a sea-king than the personification of the crossing itself.
figures.provenance: Immram Brain (Voyage of Bran, 7th-8th c.); Cormac's Glossary; Manx folklore recorded in A.W. Moore, Folk-Lore of the Isle of Man (1891).

figures.gloss: The 'Hound of Culann,' foster-son of Conchobar mac Nessa and central champion of the Ulster Cycle's Táin Bó Cúailnge; transformed in combat by the ríastrad ('warp-spasm') into a monstrous form, he single-handedly holds the ford against the army of Connacht.
figures.facet: His death scene — binding himself upright to a standing stone so he dies on his feet, facing his enemies — makes him the corpus's clearest case of defiance outlasting the body.
figures.provenance: Táin Bó Cúailnge (Book of the Dun Cow / Book of Leinster, 12th c.); Aided Con Culainn (The Death of Cú Chulainn).

figures.gloss: The divine hag of Gaelic Scotland and Ireland — the Cailleach Bhéara — who strides the land dropping stones from her creel to build mountains and islands, herds deer and storms, and rules the dark half of the year, renewing to a youthful form in some tellings.
figures.facet: Landscape as biography: unlike sky- or sea-gods defined by domain, her myth is told through the terrain itself — hills and reefs read as the literal residue of her actions.
figures.provenance: Scottish Gaelic oral tradition collected in Alexander Carmichael, Carmina Gadelica (1900); the Irish Cailleach Bhéarra lament (Duanaire Finn tradition, 9th-10th c.) mourns her lost youth across seven cycles of age.

figures.gloss: Champion of eloquence and strength among the Túatha Dé Danann, credited in Irish tradition with inventing the ogham alphabet; his Gaulish counterpart Ogmios is described by Lucian of Samosata (2nd c. CE) as an old, sun-burnt Heracles-figure leading willing followers bound by fine chains running from his tongue to their ears.
figures.facet: Lucian's vignette makes eloquence itself the weapon and the leash — persuasion literalized as chains of gold and amber, pleasant to the bound rather than degrading.
figures.provenance: Lucian of Samosata, Heracles (2nd c. CE); medieval ogham tracts and Cath Maige Tuired crediting Ogma with the script's invention.

figures.gloss: The parallel, deathless realm entered through síd-mounds, sea-mists, or western voyages — Tír na nÓg ('Land of the Young') in later Irish tradition, Annwn in Welsh; time runs differently there, as the immrama (voyage tales) and echtrai (adventure tales) insist, visitors returning to find centuries have passed.
figures.facet: A cosmology organized around permeable thresholds rather than a fixed vertical order — the Otherworld is reached sideways, through hills and across water, not by ascent or descent.
figures.provenance: Immram Brain; Echtra Chonlai; the Four Branches of the Mabinogi (Annwn episodes, 12th-13th c. Welsh manuscripts of earlier material).

figures.gloss: The year-turning night (Oct 31/Nov 1) marking the boundary between the light and dark halves of the Irish calendar year, when síd-mounds stand open and the dead, alongside Otherworld hosts, are believed to walk among the living.
figures.facet: A calendar rite that survives its cosmology: stripped of the Túatha Dé and the sídhe, its structural bones — a threshold night for the dead — persist almost unaltered as Halloween.
figures.provenance: Tochmarc Emire and other Ulster Cycle tales set at Samhain; Sanas Cormaic; Ronald Hutton, Stations of the Sun (1996), tracing the rite's Christian-era transformations.

figures.gloss: Gaulish tribal protector god ('god of the people,' from *teutā, 'tribe') named alongside Esus and Taranis in Lucan's Pharsalia (1st c. CE) as one of three Gaulish deities requiring human propitiation; the later Berne scholia on Lucan describe victims drowned in a vat in his honor.
figures.facet: One of the very few Gaulish gods reaching classical literature by his native name rather than through Roman equation — evidence surviving despite, not because of, the interpretatio Romana that usually erases native names.
figures.provenance: Lucan, Pharsalia I.444-446 (1st c. CE); Commenta Bernensia (Berne scholia on Lucan, medieval).

edges.note: Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The rigid, frontal sky-father wielding absolute atmospheric power through symbolic implements of symmetry and rotation.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Fearsome female ritual authorities steeped in the imagery of death, warfare, and the collection of the slain.
Both are feminine powers of fate and death, allotting the doom of warriors — Manat measures the appointed term as the Morrigan presides over slaughter; the goddess as apportioner of mortality.
Wheel-and-thunder gods of the western and eastern IE world; both carry the *(s)tenh2- / *Perkwunos thunder-complex, no proven common name.
Both touch the allotment of life and death, but Laima is a benevolent midwife of fate where the Morrigan is its war-shadow — a loose, divergent parallel.
A sovereign female power of the land tied to weather, omen and the punishment of oath-breakers — independent witnesses to the moral-sovereign goddess.
Both feed the late hagiographic dragon-slaying frame (Michael, the saint vs. the serpent); offered as structural parallel only, heavily Christian-mediated.

Speculative: a fierce battle-mother who scatters the enemies of her people — Sidpa Gyalmo and the Morrígan-figure as the war-band aspect of the great-mother; a loose cross-family rhyme of the martial feminine, flagged.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The mythic smith whose metallurgical mastery equips the divine hosts, dwelling in an otherworldly domain.

The battlefield goddess who feeds on the slain and grants or withholds victory — Koṟṟavai and the Morrígan as the war-mother who haunts the field of the dead, a striking structural parallel across wholly separate families.

The carrion-spirits of the battlefield, feeding on the slain where the war-goddess reigns — the pēy and the Morrígan's crows as the appetite of the field of the dead, a parallel of the same grim image.
Both are winged female death-attendants who mark rather than cause the appointed end — the Morrigan over the battlefield, Vanth at the deathbed.
Powerful, fearsome female sovereigns set against the heroes; Louhi the northern sorceress-queen and the Morrigan share menace and command, but the parallel is loose and the families unrelated.
Tutelary keepers of the wild presiding over a sacred natural order; Mielikki of the forest loosely parallels the great mother-of-the-land figures — a soft, divergent rhyme rather than a firm joint.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are Indo-European thunderers associated with a specific symbolic weapon (hammer and wheel) and invoked for the protection of the tribe.

GOLD SEAM: Both are smiths of the divine — Goibniu is the Celtic smith-god who forges weapons and brews the ale of immortality, while the Hermetic alchemist forges the Philosopher's Stone that grants eternal life.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both embody the sacred, life-giving flow of natural waters and springs in ancient European tribal religion.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: A divine smith whose forge is the center of mythical creation and sustenance.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the ancient Indo-European archetype of the divine river mother anchoring the fertility and sovereignty of the land.

Thunder as a venerated power, but Pillán diverges sharply: where Taranis is a sky-borne thunder-god, the Mapuche thunder IS the ancestral dead speaking from the volcano — the storm-wielder joint reached through genealogy, not a separate deity.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Modern Druidry directly imports and invokes the Celtic figure of Danu, elevating her as the supreme Earth Mother archetype in contemporary liturgy.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: The terrifying, weeping female specter who appears as a direct, unalterable omen of impending death.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both act as divine smiths and martial forces whose mastery over metal clears the path for civilization and war.
GOLD SEAM: Implacable, terrifying sovereign deities linked to wheels, fire, and the reception of ultimate human sacrifices in classical historiography.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Terrifying, blood-soaked goddesses of war and destruction who laugh amidst carnage and revel in the dissolution of enemies.
GOLD SEAM: The ancient European thundering sky god, wielding the terrifying acoustic and destructive power of the storm.
GOLD SEAM: The Crone aspect echoes the dark, prophetic, and death-bringing nature of the Celtic Badb.
Lugh kills his own grandfather Balor with a sling-stone at the second battle of Mag Tuired — the chaoskampf ending Fomorian dominance.
Alongside Ogma, the Dagda and Lugh jointly plan the Túatha Dé's strategy against the Fomorians in Cath Maige Tuired.
The ruling triad of Cath Maige Tuired's war-council: Dagda, Ogma, and Lugh set the Túatha Dé's strategy together.
The Mórrígan appears to Cú Chulainn as a washer at the ford prophesying his death, having earlier tested and been wounded by him in animal form during the Táin.

Scottish Highland folk tradition (Carmichael, Carmina Gadelica) pairs the Cailleach's rule over winter with Bríde/Brigid's rule over summer, the two trading sovereignty of the land around Là Fhèill Brìghde (Feb 1).
Manannán is named lord of the Otherworld isles (Emain Ablach, Tír Tairngire) the concept designates.
Samhain is the calendrical hinge on which the Otherworld's síd-mounds stand open to the living world.
At Samhain the Dagda couples with the Mórrígan at the River Unshin, an act securing the Túatha Dé's victory over the Fomorians in Cath Maige Tuired.

Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: both read through the 'Master/Mistress of Animals' template — a horned or beast-flanked figure defined by iconographic company rather than narrative — though gendered opposite and culturally unconnected.

Direct continuity of name and antlered iconography: modern Druidry's Horned God explicitly adopts the ancient Gaulish figure, adding an ecological/fertility theology the ancient evidence cannot itself support.

Both name a parallel realm reached across water where time runs differently and the dead or ancestral spirits dwell — a structural rhyme between insular Celtic and Ryukyuan cosmology, no contact attested.
battlefield spirits who choose who dies and gather what's owed from the slain — the Valkyries riding to choose for Óðinn's hall, the Mórrígan as Badb crying over the field as harbinger of death.

dossier.essence: The pre-Christian religion of the Celtic-speaking peoples of Iron Age Europe — Gaul, Britain, Ireland, Iberia, the Alpine lands, and Galatia — from roughly the Hallstatt/La Tène horizon through Romanization and Christianization. No insider account survives: the religion must be triangulated from archaeology, Greco-Roman testimony, Romano-Celtic inscriptions, and medieval Irish and Welsh literature written down centuries later by Christians.
dossier.cosmos: No unified cosmology is attested. The evidence points to sanctity concentrated in the land itself — springs, rivers, groves (nemeton), and waters receiving deliberate deposits — and, in the Insular literature, an Otherworld adjacent to this one, entered through burial mounds (síd), water, or mist. Classical authors report druidic teaching that souls do not perish but pass into other bodies (Caesar, Diodorus), a doctrine they compared to Pythagorean transmigration.
dossier.practice: In Gaul and Britain a professional priestly-juridical class, the druids, taught orally, judged disputes, and presided over sacrifice; classical sources allege human sacrifice (with evident polemical interest), and finds such as the Lindow Man bog body are argued both ways. Votive deposition — weapons, cauldrons, and metalwork sunk in water — is the best-attested ritual act archaeologically. Early Irish literature preserves the quarter-day festivals Samain, Imbolc, Beltaine, and Lugnasad, along with sacral-kingship rites; how far these generalize to the ancient Continent is uncertain.
dossier.canon: None native — the druids declined to commit their teaching to writing (Caesar, Gallic War 6.14). The sources are external: Posidonian ethnography via Caesar, Strabo, and Diodorus; epigraphy and iconography (the Coligny calendar, dedications to Taranis, Epona, Lugus); and the Christian-era Irish sagas (Táin Bó Cúailnge, Lebor Gabála Érenn) and the Welsh Mabinogi.
dossier.reflects: Sanctity located in landscape rather than text — and a standing demonstration of how little of a purely oral religion survives except through the accounts of outsiders and successors.
sources: Miranda Green, 'The Gods of the Celts' (1986)
Ronald Hutton, 'Blood and Mistletoe: The History of the Druids in Britain' (2009)
